learn.title: Hydrogen Fuel Cells: Clean Power for Transport
learn.intro: Fuel cells offer a powerful alternative to heavy batteries for long-haul transport. By converting chemical energy directly into electricity, they allow vehicles to travel further and refuel faster than traditional EVs, making them critical for decarbonizing trucking, shipping, and aviation where battery weight is a limiting factor in the global transition to net-zero emissions.
learn.c1_text: A fuel cell acts like a battery that never runs out as long as it has fuel. It combines hydrogen and oxygen to create electricity. Think of it as a reverse water-splitting process: instead of using energy to split water, it joins hydrogen and oxygen to release energy, with only pure water vapor as the exhaust.
learn.c2_text: The heart of the cell is the PEM, a specialized membrane that acts like a strict security guard. It only allows positively charged protons to pass through while forcing electrons to travel through an external circuit. This flow of electrons is exactly what creates the electric current that powers the vehicle's motor.
learn.c3_text: India is pivoting toward Green Hydrogen to reduce its massive crude oil import bill. The government is focusing on fuel cell buses and heavy-duty trucks to clean up logistics corridors, integrating these cells with domestic electrolyzer manufacturing to create a self-reliant hydrogen ecosystem for the transport sector.
learn.stat1: Emissions: 0g CO2 per km during operation
learn.stat2: Refuel time: 3-5 minutes for heavy trucks
learn.stat3: Energy density: Hydrogen has 3x energy per kg than diesel
learn.stat4: Efficiency: Fuel cells reach 40-60% electrical efficiency

learn.title: Wave Energy Converters: Harvesting the Ocean
learn.intro: The ocean is the world's largest untapped energy reservoir. Wave energy converters capture the kinetic and potential energy of surface waves, providing a more predictable power source than wind or solar. Harnessing this rhythmic energy is key to diversifying the renewable mix and providing stable power to coastal communities and offshore industries.
learn.c1_text: Point absorbers are floating buoys that move up and down with the waves. Imagine a giant piston in a cylinder; as the buoy bobs, it pushes a hydraulic ram or linear generator. This vertical motion is converted into electricity, capturing energy from waves coming from any direction across the ocean surface.
learn.c2_text: Attenuators are long, snake-like structures that flex at joints as waves pass. Alternatively, Oscillating Water Columns use a chamber where waves push air through a turbine. It's like a giant set of lungs breathing in and out, spinning a turbine both when the water rises and falls.
learn.c3_text: With a coastline of over 7,500 km, India has immense potential for marine energy. Research focuses on the Gujarat and Tamil Nadu coasts, where high wave energy density can support hybrid offshore platforms combining wind and wave energy to stabilize power delivery to the national grid.
learn.stat1: Global potential: Estimated 2-3 TW of wave power
learn.stat2: Energy density: Water is 800x denser than air
learn.stat3: Capacity factor: Wave energy is more consistent than wind

learn.title: Renewable Purchase Obligations for DISCOMs
learn.intro: Renewable Purchase Obligations (RPO) are the primary policy levers used to force the adoption of green energy. By mandating that power distribution companies (DISCOMs) source a specific percentage of their electricity from renewable sources, governments ensure a guaranteed market for wind and solar developers, accelerating the phase-out of coal-fired power.
learn.c1_text: RPO is like a mandatory 'green quota' for electricity sellers. If a DISCOM must meet a 20% RPO, it means 20% of the total power it sells must come from renewables. If they fail, they must pay a penalty or buy Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) to make up the gap.
learn.c2_text: When a DISCOM cannot build its own plants, it uses RECs. Think of an REC as a 'green voucher' issued to a wind farm for every MWh produced. The DISCOM buys these vouchers to prove they have supported renewable growth, even if the power was generated far away.
learn.c3_text: Indian DISCOMs often struggle with RPO compliance due to financial stress. The MNRE implements strict targets to push these utilities toward green energy, but the transition is balanced with 'must-run' status for renewables to ensure green power is prioritized over coal during peak demand.
learn.stat1: India: RPO targets vary by state (approx 20-25%)
learn.stat2: Penalty: Non-compliance leads to financial penalties
learn.stat3: REC: 1 REC = 1 MWh of renewable energy
learn.stat4: Goal: Net Zero by 2070 requires 100% RPO eventually

learn.title: BIPV: Integrating Solar into Architecture
learn.intro: Building Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV) transform a building's skin into a power plant. Instead of adding panels on top of a roof, BIPV replaces traditional building materials—like windows, facades, and shingles—with solar-generating materials. This eliminates the need for extra land and turns urban jungles into active energy producers.
learn.c1_text: BIPV is like replacing your house's wallpaper with a solar panel. Instead of a heavy glass window, you use a semi-transparent solar pane. The window still lets in light, but it also captures photons to generate electricity, turning every square inch of the building's exterior into a generator.
learn.c2_text: Passive BIPV provides shade or insulation while generating power. Active BIPV is fully integrated into the electrical system to power the building's HVAC and lighting. This reduces 'transmission loss' because the energy is consumed exactly where it is produced, rather than traveling miles from a power plant.
learn.c3_text: In dense Indian cities like Mumbai or Bangalore, land for solar farms is scarce. BIPV allows high-rise commercial buildings to meet their energy needs using vertical facades. This aligns with India's 'Smart Cities Mission' to reduce the carbon footprint of urban infrastructure through integrated green technology.
learn.stat2: Space saving: 0 additional land required for generation
learn.stat3: Cost: BIPV reduces long-term cladding and energy costs
learn.stat4: India: Growing adoption in commercial LEED-certified buildings

learn.title: Demand Response: Balancing the Power Grid
learn.intro: The power grid must always be perfectly balanced; supply must equal demand every second. Demand Response (DR) manages this balance not by increasing supply, but by adjusting demand. By incentivizing large consumers to lower their usage during peaks, DR prevents blackouts and reduces the need for expensive, polluting 'peaker' power plants.
learn.c1_text: Demand response is like a 'virtual power plant.' Instead of turning on a new generator, the grid operator asks factories to turn off non-essential machines for an hour. Reducing 100MW of demand is mathematically the same as generating 100MW of new power, but it's faster and cleaner.
learn.c2_text: Price-based DR uses 'Time of Use' pricing—electricity is expensive at 6 PM and cheap at 2 AM. Incentive-based DR pays companies a flat fee to cut power during emergencies. It's like a reward system: 'We will pay you $1,000 if you shut down your AC for two hours.'
learn.c3_text: India's textile and steel industries are prime candidates for DR. By shifting heavy industrial loads to nighttime (off-peak), these companies reduce grid stress. This is critical for India as it integrates more variable wind and solar power, which require flexible demand to maintain stability.
learn.stat1: Peak Reduction: Can lower peak demand by 10-15%
learn.stat2: Cost: DR is often 3x cheaper than building new peaker plants
learn.stat3: Response time: DR can react in seconds to minutes
learn.stat4: Impact: Reduces reliance on diesel backup generators

learn.title: National Green Hydrogen Mission: India's Roadmap
learn.intro: The National Green Hydrogen Mission is India's strategic blueprint to become a global hub for the production and export of green hydrogen. By using renewable energy to split water via electrolysis, India aims to decarbonize 'hard-to-abate' sectors like steel and refineries, ensuring energy security and reducing dependence on fossil fuel imports.
learn.c1_text: Hydrogen is 'green' only if it's made using renewable energy. Think of it as a clean battery: solar power runs an electrolyzer to split water (H2O) into hydrogen and oxygen. The hydrogen is stored as a gas and burned or used in a fuel cell, leaving only water behind.
learn.c2_text: The Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT) provides financial incentives for two things: manufacturing electrolyzers and producing green hydrogen. It's a 'carrot' approach, giving subsidies to companies to lower the initial high cost of technology and make green hydrogen competitive with grey hydrogen.
learn.c3_text: India targets 5 MMT (Million Metric Tonnes) of green hydrogen production per year by 2030. This massive scale-up is supported by the creation of 'Hydrogen Hubs'—clusters where production and consumption (like a refinery and a port) are located together to minimize transport costs.
learn.stat1: Target: 5 MMT green hydrogen production by 2030
learn.stat2: Energy: Addition of 125 GW of renewable energy capacity
learn.stat4: Investment: Potential investment of ₹8 lakh crore
